56541.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term refers to a verse‑containing sutta in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN 55.51).

60528.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Samphassasutta is the sutta in the Uppādasaṃyutta dealing with sense contact (samphassa).

70281.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
vedanāsutta 3 is the third discourse on feeling in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (Saṃyutta 45). The compound consists of vedanā “feeling” and sutta “discourse”, forming a neuter noun.

57301.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the third discourse on intention (sañcetanā) in the Saṃyutta Nikāya 27, classified as a neuter noun compound.

5227.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
This sutta, part of the Indriya Saṃyutta (SN 48.62), deals with the underlying tendencies (anuyā) of the senses. The title literally means “the discourse on underlying tendencies” and is a neuter noun compound anusa + sutta.

35396.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta name 'Nakhasikha' is a kammadhāraya compound meaning roughly 'the sutta of the nail‑peak'. It appears in SN 56.51 within the Saccasaṃyutta.

7213.meaning_1: having pulled out; having plucked out; having uprooted
7213.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
abbāhitvā is the absolutive participle of abbahati, a variant spelling of abbahitvāna. In the sentence it functions as “having pulled out” (causative sense) and matches the meaning of the related form abbuhetvā.
7213.example_1: taṃ nāgā upanissāya viharanti. te taṃ sarasiṃ ogāhetvā soṇḍāya bhisamuḷālaṃ abbuhetvā [abbhūhetvā, abbāhitvā (mahāva. 278)] suvikkhālitaṃ vikkhāletvā akaddamaṃ saṅkhāditvā [saṅkharitvā] ajjhoharanti. tesaṃ taṃ vaṇṇāya ceva hoti balāya ca, na ca tatonidānaṃ maraṇaṃ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṃ vā dukkhaṃ.

56736.meaning_1: having gathered together; having banded together
56736.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN20.9 the absolute participle saṅkharitvā introduces a prior action “having gathered together” before the main verb ajjhoharanti (“they seize”). It functions as a non‑finite verb form indicating a completed collective action.
56736.example_1: taṃ nāgā upanissāya viharanti. te taṃ sarasiṃ ogāhetvā soṇḍāya bhisamuḷālaṃ abbuhetvā [abbhūhetvā, abbāhitvā (mahāva. 278)] suvikkhālitaṃ vikkhāletvā akaddamaṃ saṅkhāditvā [saṅkharitvā] ajjhoharanti. tesaṃ taṃ vaṇṇāya ceva hoti balāya ca, na ca tatonidānaṃ maraṇaṃ vā nigacchanti maraṇamattaṃ vā dukkhaṃ.

48458.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Biḷārasutta is a neuter noun denoting a discourse (sutta) that appears in Saṃyutta Nikāya 20. The name is a compound of biḷāra (a kind of fish, from Sanskrit biḍāla) and sutta “hymn” or “scripture”; the meaning lit field gives a generic “discourse on the aggregates”.

36843.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “so tatonidānaṃ maraṇampi nigacchi maraṇamattampi dukkhaṃ”, nigacchi means “goes down” i.e., “falls” or “ends in death”, indicating the decline into death and suffering.
36843.sutta_1: so tatonidānaṃ maraṇampi nigacchi maraṇamattampi dukkhaṃ.
36843.example_1: resulting in death and deadly pain.

33440.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the second Siṅgāla discourse (Saṃyutta Nikāya 20). The compound dutiyasiṅgālasutta is a neuter noun meaning “second Siṅgāla sutta”.

23152.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Kolita is a noun meaning “deed” or “action”; combined with sutta (discourse) it denotes the discourse on deeds, found as SN 21.1 in the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

15611.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN21.2, upatissasutta refers to the Upatissa Sutta, a discourse named after the monk Upatissa and belonging to the Saṃyutta Nikāya 21.

25443.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term refers to the specific sutta in Saṃyutta Nikāya 21 dealing with the “pot” (ghaṭa); in this context it is simply the name of that discourse.

69292.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sutta the verb visujjhi means “purifies” or “cleanses”, referring to the Buddha purifying his divine eye (dibba‑cakkhu) toward the listener.
69292.sutta_1: api ca me yāvatā bhagavā ettāvatā dibbacakkhu visujjhi dibbā ca sotadhātu.
69292.example_1: Rather, the Buddha cleared his clairvoyance and clairaudience towards me,

56085.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of the sutta the noun loṇaghaṭā (locative loṇaghaṭāya) denotes a “salt pot”, used metaphorically to compare a single grain of salt to a large mound of salt, emphasizing the speaker’s humility.
56085.sutta_1: “seyyathāpi, āvuso, mahatiyā loṇaghaṭāya parittā loṇasakkharāya yāvadeva upanikkhepanamattāya;
56085.example_1: “Reverend, next to Venerable Sāriputta I’m like a few grains of salt next to a mound of salt.

56090.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the contextual sentence the word conveys the idea of a few grains of salt, highlighting the speaker’s modest size compared to a large heap of salt.
56090.sutta_1: “seyyathāpi, āvuso, mahatiyā loṇaghaṭāya parittā loṇasakkharāya yāvadeva upanikkhepanamattāya;
56090.example_1: “Reverend, next to Venerable Sāriputta I’m like a few grains of salt next to a mound of salt.

35938.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Navasutta is the “new sutta” in SN21.4, indicating a discourse among the bhikkhu‑saṃyutta collection.

62885.meaning_1: by being slack; due to laziness
62885.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound sithilamārabbha expresses the cause “slackness” or laziness. In the sentence “nayidaṃ sithilamārabbha” it is translated “not by being slack”, indicating that the action is not due to laziness.

39928.meaning_1: son of Pañcāla; name of a monk
39928.meaning_lit: belonging to the Pañcāla people; (or) Pañcālā's son
39928.family_set: names of monks; names of arahants
39928.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the term refers to the monk Pañcālaputta, literally “son of Pañcāla”, a proper name for an arahant. It is a typical chaṭṭhī tappurisa compound formed from the place‑name Pañcāla and the suffix ‑putta “son”.
39928.sutta_1: tena kho pana samayena āyasmā visākho pañcālaputto upaṭṭhānasālāyaṃ bhikkhū dhammiyā kathāya sandasseti samādapeti samuttejeti sampahaṃseti, poriyā vācāya vissaṭṭhāya anelagalāya atthassa viññāpaniyā pariyāpannāya anissitāya.
39928.example_1: Now at that time Venerable Visākha, Pañcāli’s son, was educating, encouraging, firing up, and inspiring the mendicants in the assembly hall with a Dhamma talk. His words were polished, clear, articulate, expressing the meaning, comprehensive, and independent.

64257.meaning_lit: well spoken words as a banner
64257.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN21.7:6.3 the phrase “subhāsitadhajā isayo” lik the seers’ banner to be the well‑spoken words they uphold.
64257.example_1: Words well spoken are the seers’ banner,

52230.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term denotes the son of one’s maternal aunt, i.e., the mother’s sister’s son, which in the context refers to the Buddha’s cousin on his mother’s side.
52230.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā nando bhagavato mātucchāputto ākoṭitapaccākoṭitāni cīvarāni pārupitvā akkhīni añjetvā acchaṃ pattaṃ gahetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinnaṃ kho āyasmantaṃ nandaṃ bhagavā etadavoca:
52230.example_1: Then Venerable Nanda—the Buddha’s cousin on his mother’s side—dressed in nicely pressed and ironed robes, applied eyeshadow, and took a glazed bowl. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him:

1493.meaning_1: having put on eyeshadow; having applied collyrium
1493.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage, añjetvā indicates that Nanda had applied collyrium (eye makeup) before taking the bowl, marking a preparatory action prior to approaching the Buddha.
1493.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā nando bhagavato mātucchāputto ākoṭitapaccākoṭitāni cīvarāni pārupitvā akkhīni añjetvā acchaṃ pattaṃ gahetvā yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi. ekamantaṃ nisinnaṃ kho āyasmantaṃ nandaṃ bhagavā etadavoca:
1493.example_1: Then Venerable Nanda—the Buddha’s cousin on his mother’s side—dressed in nicely pressed and ironed robes, applied eyeshadow, and took a glazed bowl. He went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. The Buddha said to him:

1635.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound consists of the past participle “aññāta” (known) and the noun “uñcha” (food), together conveying the notion of taking or feeding on food that is offered by strangers, i.e., gathering food from strangers.
1635.example_1: feeding on scraps offered by strangers,

30866.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term refers to the discourse of the monk Tissa, a sutta in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN 21.9).

46115.meaning_1: cousin on father’s side (son of father’s brother)
46115.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sutta the term refers to the Buddha’s paternal cousin, i.e., the son of his father’s brother.
46115.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā tisso bhagavato pitucchāputto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi dukkhī dummano assūni pavattayamāno. atha kho bhagavā āyasmantaṃ tissaṃ etadavoca:
46115.example_1: Then Venerable Tissa—the Buddha’s cousin on his father’s side—went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. He was miserable and sad, with tears flowing. Then the Buddha said to him:

44842.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN 21.9:1.2, pavattayamāna describes the tears that are flowing, i.e., the tears are in the state of moving forward.
44842.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā tisso bhagavato pitucchāputto yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami; upasaṅkamitvā bhagavantaṃ abhivādetvā ekamantaṃ nisīdi dukkhī dummano assūni pavattayamāno. atha kho bhagavā āyasmantaṃ tissaṃ etadavoca:
44842.example_1: Then Venerable Tissa—the Buddha’s cousin on his father’s side—went to the Buddha, bowed, and sat down to one side. He was miserable and sad, with tears flowing. Then the Buddha said to him:

57335.meaning_1: laughed at; taunted; made fun of
57335.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The verb is a compound of sañjambhari (a noun meaning mockery or laughter) and the causal verb akāsi “made”. In the context it conveys “to mock or jeer at someone”.
57335.sutta_1: “tathā hi pana maṃ, bhante, bhikkhū samantā vācāyasannitodakena sañjambharimakaṃsū”ti.
57335.example_1: “Sir, it’s because the mendicants beset me on all sides with sneering and jeering.”

23065.meaning_1: for removing anger, conceit and denigration
23065.meaning_lit: for the purpose of removing anger, conceit and denigration
23065.construction: kodha + māna + makkha + vinaya + attha + aṃ
23065.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The compound literally means “for the purpose of removing anger, conceit and denigration”, which fits the context of spiritual practice.
23065.example_1: For this spiritual life is lived

31625.meaning_1: discourse on the names of the elders
31625.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The name refers to the sutta that records the names of the senior monks (theras). It is a kammadhāraya compound composed of thera + nāma + sutta, meaning “discourse on the names of the elders”.

31624.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context the word functions as an adjective meaning 'senior in name only', i.e., a monk who is called a thera but lacks the senior status. The compound is formed from thera (elder) + nāmaka (named).
31624.sutta_1: tena kho pana samayena aññataro bhikkhu theranāmako ekavihārī ceva hoti ekavihārassa ca vaṇṇavādī.
31624.example_1: Now at that time there was a certain mendicant who was Senior in name only. He lived alone and praised living alone.

51661.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to the sutta “Mahākappinasutta” (Great Kappina Discourse) found in Saṃyutta Nikāya 21, SN 21.11. It is a neuter noun compound of mahā “great”, kappina (a proper name), and sutta “discourse”. No further derivational or root information applies.

18622.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage the adjective odātaka describes a monk as “white” or “pale”, matching the English rendering “white, thin”. The form is built from the root odā plus the adjectival suffix –ka.
18622.sutta_1: “passatha no tumhe, bhikkhave, etaṃ bhikkhuṃ āgacchantaṃ odātakaṃ tanukaṃ tuṅganāsikan”ti?
18622.example_1: “Mendicants, do you see that monk coming—white, thin, with a pointy nose?”

30948.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Compound of tuṅga “nose” + nāsika “related to nose”, literally “nose‑pointed”; in the Vinaya passage it describes a monk as thin, white and having a pointed nose.
30948.sutta_1: “passatha no tumhe, bhikkhave, etaṃ bhikkhuṃ āgacchantaṃ odātakaṃ tanukaṃ tuṅganāsikan”ti?
30948.example_1: “Mendicants, do you see that monk coming—white, thin, with a pointy nose?”

58924.meaning_1: throughout the day and night; continually; all the time
58924.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence 'atha sabbamahorattiṃ', the adverb means 'throughout day and night' or 'continuously', derived from the compound sabba + ahorattiṃ (all + day‑night).
58924.example_1: But all day and all night,

61471.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Sahāyakasutta is the “Discourse on the Aggregates”, a sutta (Saṃyutta Nikāya 21) whose name is a compound of sahāyaka “helper, companion” and sutta “discourse”. It is a neuter noun, hence Lemma‑2 ends in –ṃ.

60165.construction:
saṃ + e + ta + ika
sameta + ika
60165.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “cirarattaṃ sametikā” the adjective sametika conveys the idea of something that has been together for a long time, i.e., “joined, associated”. The form sametikā agrees with the implicit noun and reflects the core meaning “going together, united”.
60165.example_1: have been together for a long time.

48539.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence the adjective modifies dhamm, indicating the teaching that has been proclaimed by the Buddha.
48539.example_1: the teaching proclaimed by the Buddha.

31623.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage 'theranāmo ca kappino', the term denotes the elder monk Kappino; it is a compound of thera (elder) and nāma (name), essentially meaning 'senior' or 'elder by name'.

37205.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The term denotes the second book of the Saṃyutta Nikāya, literally “the Nidāna‑vagga Saṃyutta Pāḷi”, indicating a collection of discourses on causality.
37205.example_1: The Book of Causality is finished.

23469.meaning_1: Book 3 of the Saṃyutta Nikāya; Book of the Aggregates
23469.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the citation 'khandhavaggo tatiyo', the noun is in the nominative plural, indicating 'the third book of the aggregates' within the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

35368.meaning_1: group of the fathers of Nakula
35368.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In SN 22.11:1.16 the term denotes the first “vagga” (section) named for the fathers of Nakula, i.e. the class of those whose father is Nakula.

35365.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The entry refers to a sutta (discourse) named after the father of Nakula; it is a neuter noun compound typical of sutta titles in the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

58863.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Used in SN 21.4:5.4, it denotes the act of liberating all suffering, i.e., “the release from all suffering”.

63709.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The sutta is named after the monk Sujāta; it is a discourse (sutta) concerning him.

55464.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The headword is a sutta name in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN21). It is a compound of lakuṇḍaka “lump/stone”, bhaddiya “good/beneficial”, and sutta “discourse”. The meaning reflects the title of the discourse.

55462.family_set: names of monks; names of arahants
55462.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
The name appears in vocative as lakuṇḍakabhaddiyo, indicating a respectful address to the monk “lakuṇḍakabhaddiya”, literally “lucky dwarf”. It is a compound of lakuṇḍa “dwarf” and bhadda “good”.
55462.sutta_1: atha kho āyasmā lakuṇḍakabhaddiyo yena bhagavā tenupasaṅkami.
55462.example_1: Then Venerable Bhaddiya the Dwarf went to see the Buddha.

43837.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the context of SN 21.6:1.5 the adjective “paribhūtarūpa” describes a monk who is despised (by the mendicants). The term is a kammadhāraya compound of “paribhūta” (despised) and “rūpa” (form). The case ending is accusative, hence plus_case is +acc.
43837.sutta_1: “passatha no tumhe, bhikkhave, etaṃ bhikkhuṃ āgacchantaṃ dubbaṇṇaṃ duddasikaṃ okoṭimakaṃ bhikkhūnaṃ paribhūtarūpan”ti?
43837.example_1: “Mendicants, do you see this monk coming—ugly, unsightly, deformed, and despised by the mendicants?”

9535.meaning_1: not attained before; not previously achieved
9535.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the passage it qualifies 'bhikkhunā', meaning a bhikkhuni who has not previously attained that particular attainment.
9535.sutta_1: “eso kho, bhikkhave, bhikkhu mahiddhiko mahānubhāvo, na ca sā samāpatti sulabharūpā yā tena bhikkhunā asamāpannapubbā.
9535.example_1: “That mendicant is very mighty and powerful. It’s not easy to find an attainment that he has not already attained.

44592.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
In the sentence “hatthayo pasadā migā” the word pasadā is the instrumental singular of the masculine noun pasāda, meaning “deer”, here understood as “spotted deer”. The lemma_2 is corrected to the nominative singular pasāda.

31083.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Used in the sentence “natthi kāyasmiñ tulyatā” meaning “equality” or “sameness”, indicating that all are alike in fearing the lion.

69230.comment:
openrouter: openrouter/aurora-alpha
Visākhā Sutta is the 7th sutta of the Bhikkhusaṃyutta, dealing with the Visākhā tree; the literal translation follows the standard “discourse on the aggregates”.
